title: Polymer clay button set
subtitle: A set of matching buttons baked hard for use on handmade garments
excerpt: Polymer clay buttons are cut from a rolled slab, pierced with two or four holes, and baked at 130°C for 20 minutes. They are durable enough for regular sewing use, hand-washable, and can be made in any colour to match a specific garment. No kiln is needed. A set of eight to ten buttons for a cardigan takes under an hour to make.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay button technique. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A PVC-based modelling clay that hardens permanently in a home oven at 130°C. Buttons made from it are durable and hand-washable.
Baking polymer clay until it sets hard. Buttons cure fully in 20 minutes at 130°C.

content.text: Polymer clay
buttons are practical rather than purely decorative.
Cured

polymer clay is hard, smooth, and holds a sewing hole without cracking under normal button use. Buttons can be hand-washed but not machine-washed or tumble-dried. The main value over commercial buttons is colour matching: mix clay to the exact colour needed for a garment.

attrs.title: Button size and thickness
attrs.body: Standard cardigan buttons are 15 to 20 mm in diameter. Roll the slab 4 mm thick, thinner than 4 mm and the buttons are fragile; thicker and they sit awkwardly on the fabric. For a consistent set, cut a circle template from card and use it as a guide for every button.

content.text: What you need
Making the buttons
Step 1: Condition and roll the slab
Condition the clay until fully pliable. Roll to 4 mm thick on a smooth flat surface. Roll in multiple directions to keep the slab even.
Step 2: Add texture (optional)

If the buttons are to have a textured face, press a stamp, piece of lace, or a fabric with an open weave into the slab now. Apply even pressure across the full area so the texture depth is consistent across all buttons cut from this slab.
Step 3: Cut the buttons

Use the card template as a guide. Press the fettling knife straight down around the circumference of the template in short cuts rather than dragging. Rotate the template and cut fresh sections. Lift out the circle. Smooth the cut edge with a damp finger. Repeat to cut all buttons from the same slab, keeping them close-packed to reduce waste.
Step 4: Pierce the holes

Pierce two holes in each button using the needle tool, positioned symmetrically in the centre of the button with 4 to 5 mm between them. For a four-hole button, add a second pair at 90 degrees to the first pair. Pierce from the face straight through to the back in a single firm movement. The holes must be large enough for standard sewing thread to pass through doubled.
Step 5: Bake

Place buttons on baking paper on a dedicated tray. Preheat the oven to 130°C, confirmed with the oven thermometer. Bake for 20 minutes. Cool on the tray. Test one button by trying to flex it, fully cured buttons are hard and do not flex. If still slightly flexible, return to the oven for a further 10 minutes.
Variations
Apply millefiori cane slices to the button face before cutting for a patterned surface.
Roll together two slightly different shades for a subtle variegated colour.
Common problems

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems when making polymer clay buttons.

items.symptom: Buttons in the set are slightly different sizes
items.cause: Template not used, or slab was not rolled evenly before cutting.
items.fix: Always use a card template and cut all buttons from the same slab without re-rolling. Offcuts can be recombined and used for another project.

items.symptom: Holes too small for sewing thread
items.cause: Needle tool too thin, or clay shrank around the hole during baking.
items.fix: Polymer clay does not shrink in baking, so if the holes are too small they were not pierced large enough. Re-pierce before baking: once cured, drilling through polymer clay is possible but slow.
